summary: I was born and raised in Guatemala, growing up during a time of great turmoil and armed conflict that lasted 36 years. My family was middle class and somewhat removed from the violence, but I witnessed firsthand the deep social injustices, racism, and poverty affecting much of the country, especially in rural indigenous areas. Despite the challenges, I had access to education and developed a strong sense of social justice. Guatemala is where my roots are, and even after moving abroad, I carry its culture and people in my heart.

The Guatemalan Civil War began almost the year I was born and lasted 36 years, marked by oppression, violence, and death. I lived through bombings, gunfire, and constant fear on the streets, while my family stayed away from political involvement. As a student at San Carlos University, I was labeled a terrorist simply for being associated with leftist students, even though I was not a militant. The war shaped my understanding of injustice and motivated me to pursue education and work in development to help my country.
cues: started almost the year I was born

summary: I attended San Carlos University during the height of the civil war, where students were often linked to leftist movements and considered terrorists by the government. Being a student there was challenging because many classmates were kidnapped or killed, yet I persisted and graduated. The university exposed me to political activism and social realities, even though my family was not involved. It was a formative place that shaped my commitment to social justice and understanding of Guatemala’s complex political landscape.

The Peace Accords signed in 1996 marked the official end of the armed conflict, but violence and delinquency actually increased afterward. My husband and I were concerned about raising our children in such an environment, which motivated me to seek educational opportunities abroad. The accords were a turning point for Guatemala, but the country still faced many challenges in achieving true peace and stability. They influenced my decision to move my family to the United States in 2004 for a safer future.

In 2004, my family and I moved to the United States after I received a scholarship to pursue higher education. Starting over as an immigrant was difficult—learning the language, adapting to a new culture, and balancing family responsibilities was overwhelming at times. Although our lives changed significantly, with graduations and new beginnings, our hearts remained connected to Guatemala. The United States gave me opportunities to grow professionally and personally, but I never forgot where I came from.
